description: Trigger discrimination for the cross-tenant monitoring-console DESIGN owner (this skill) vs its three seams: SEEING vs ACTING (admin-console-architect owns cross-tenant actions, impersonation, break-glass ELEVATION, the operator control-plane; this skill owns the read console and the narrower break-glass CONTENT reveal), DESIGN vs OPERATE (observability-operator wires/operates the telemetry backend; this skill designs the in-product console), and CONSOLE vs FEED (slo-reliability-architect decides what pages, audit-log-architect owns the audit substrate, security-logging-alerting-architect owns detection coverage, synthetic-monitoring-architect owns probes, usage-metering-and-cost-attribution-pipeline-designer owns the metering ETL, authorization-matrix-designer owns the authz policy). The discriminating trigger token is observability/monitoring/see: a console to SEE platform state lands here; a console to ACT, a backend to WIRE, or a feed to PRODUCE lands with its owner.

cases.prompt: Design a superadmin monitoring console for our multi-tenant platform — the operators want to see signups, database performance, security events, and cost across all tenants in one place, and we need to know how to secure that god-view.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — 'monitoring console to SEE across tenants' is this skill's owned surface. admin-console-architect explicitly excludes telemetry ('seeing is not acting'); observability-operator operates backends and designs no console; slo-reliability-architect decides what pages, not what a console shows.
cases.reason: The cross-tenant observability/monitoring console's design — panels, IA, and the read-security model — is this skill's owned deliverable; the three neighbors each point at another for exactly this request.

cases.prompt: What should my superadmin panel show, and how do I make sure it can't be abused? We want a platform-health dashboard: open incidents, denied logins, audit activity, DB capacity.
cases.note: The what-to-show + how-to-secure-the-READ question. Which security events to DETECT would be security-logging-alerting-architect; here the events exist and the question is the console that displays them.
cases.reason: Panel selection with restraint plus the cross-tenant read-security model (registry, three-layer re-check, read-only-by-default, denied-access-as-metric) is this skill's core.

cases.prompt: Add impersonation and support actions to our internal admin console — support agents need to log in as a customer to reproduce bugs, retry stuck jobs, and issue refunds, with break-glass elevation for the dangerous ones.
cases.note: SEEING-vs-ACTING SEAM — impersonation, support ACTIONS, the operator control-plane, and break-glass ELEVATION (time-boxed approved access to ACT) are the action surface. This skill's break-glass clause is the narrower CONTENT reveal in the read path; the two are complementary, not interchangeable.
cases.reason: admin-console-architect owns the surface operators act THROUGH — cross-tenant write actions, impersonation with hard boundaries, elevation, the control-plane.

cases.prompt: Instrument our checkout service and wire up dashboards and alert rules in our observability backend so the on-call can see error rates and latency.
cases.note: DESIGN-vs-OPERATE SEAM — instrumenting services and creating/editing dashboards and alert rules in a Grafana-class backend is stack OPERATION (manual-only). No in-product cross-tenant console is being designed.
cases.reason: observability-operator instruments and operates the telemetry stack; this skill only designs the in-product console that surfaces already-produced telemetry.

cases.prompt: Define our SLOs and decide what should page the on-call versus open a ticket — we keep alerting on infrastructure noise instead of what users feel.
cases.note: CONSOLE-vs-FEED SEAM — deciding SLIs/SLOs, error budgets, and page-vs-ticket is the reliability DESIGN that feeds one panel family. The console displays its outcomes; it never derives them.
cases.reason: slo-reliability-architect owns what matters and what pages; this skill only places the resulting reliability signals in a console panel.

cases.prompt: Design our audit log — the event taxonomy, the record schema, tamper-evidence, and how long we retain audit data.
cases.note: CONSOLE-vs-FEED SEAM — the audit SUBSTRATE (taxonomy, schema, integrity, retention) is the feed. This skill contributes only the platform-tier read rules and the admin-readable viewer panel over that substrate.
cases.reason: audit-log-architect owns the audit system's design; the console skill composes it as a panel feed.

cases.prompt: Which security events should we be logging and alerting on — failed logins, privilege changes, injection attempts? Set the detection coverage and thresholds.
cases.note: CONSOLE-vs-FEED SEAM — detection coverage and alert rules PRODUCE the security signals. The console only displays them (and adds denied-superadmin-access as one displayed metric).
cases.reason: security-logging-alerting-architect owns what to detect and when to alert; the console skill owns where those signals appear and who may read them cross-tenant.

cases.prompt: Set up scheduled synthetic probes against production — which journeys to probe, how often, and how to keep the probes from mutating real data.
cases.note: CONSOLE-vs-FEED SEAM — the probe catalog and prod-safety contract are the feed. The console skill displays probe RESULTS in its verification panels.
cases.reason: synthetic-monitoring-architect owns black-box production probing; this skill renders its outcomes as posture.
